reasoning: The informal statement asks to prove $|a_1| + |2a_2| + \dots + |na_n| \leq 1$, i.e., the sum of absolute values $\sum_i |i \cdot a_i| \leq 1$. The Lean statement instead asserts `abs (∑ i, i * a i) ≤ 1`, i.e., the absolute value of the sum $|\sum_i i \cdot a_i| \leq 1$. By the triangle inequality, the informal claim implies the Lean claim but not conversely (e.g., $a_1=1, a_2=-1$ gives $\sum|i a_i|=3$ but $|\sum i a_i|=1$). The Lean version is actually a much easier statement — equivalent to $|f'(0)| \leq 1$ — whereas the original Putnam problem's substance lies in bounding the sum of the absolute values. Hence the Lean formalization is strictly logically weaker.
